g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore how users interact with operating systems through three main interfaces: CLI, GUI, and Touch-Screen.
There are three ways users interact with an OS: text commands via CLI, visual point-and-click via GUI, and gestures via Touch-Screen.
Programs interact with the OS through system calls, which provide the programmatic interface. This is different from user interfaces.
g.text: System Calls provide the interface for programs to request OS services.
Examples: open(), read(), write(), fork(), exec()

g.data-narration: The command interpreter (shell) receives user commands, interprets them, and passes them to the OS for execution. Output is then displayed.
Commands can be built into the shell or implemented as separate program files. The UNIX approach uses separate programs for flexibility.
g.text: Shell contains code for each command
New commands = new files (no shell change)

g.data-narration: In UNIX, when you type 'rm file.txt', the shell searches for the 'rm' program, loads it, and executes it with the filename as parameter.
There are several types of shells: Bourne Shell (sh), C Shell (csh), Korn Shell (ksh), and the popular Bourne Again Shell (bash).
Modern shells offer command history, tab completion, piping, redirection, scripting, aliases, and wildcards for powerful automation.
GUI uses the desktop metaphor - real-world concepts like desktops, windows, folders, and icons make computing intuitive for users.
g.text: GUI uses familiar real-world concepts to make computing intuitive:

g.data-narration: GUI uses mouse, keyboard, and trackpad as input devices. The mouse enables pointing, clicking, and dragging operations.
GUI was invented at Xerox PARC in 1973, popularized by Apple Macintosh in 1984, followed by Microsoft Windows in 1985.
GUI consists of menu bars, icons, windows, buttons, scrollbars, and taskbars that users interact with to control applications.
g.text: Menu Bar: File | Edit | View | Help

g.data-narration: Touch screens use gestures: tap selects, swipe scrolls, pinch zooms out, spread zooms in, long press opens context menus.
